paragraphs.body: The breeze is brushing against my cheeks, and I close my eyes and feel the smell of rain flowing in the air. The moon is already out, and the bright moonlight shines on the road ahead like the sun. Every blade of grass sways with the wind, and insects flutter beside the faintly glowing garden lights. “Finally home,” I say. Nobody is answering me, and yet, that’s what I want. The car, which is usually parked in our parking lot, is gone. I take a deep breath, walking past the flowers and grass. A smile gradually appears on my face as I walk into the house with keys in hand. “Mom?”, I yell at the empty house, but no one replies. Yes, yes, yes, my heart starts beating. Finally, I can be left alone! I quickly run past the stairs with the humming coming out of my throat. I close my door and open my computer. Finally, no one can stop me from playing video games. My fingers are dancing on the keyboard and my eyes are glued to the screen. Video games are stuck in my brain. I can see nothing else but games on the screen. The galaxy, the planets, adventures in abandoned factories, traveling through the starry night sky–all these things that cannot be seen in real life appear in front of me so easily.

annotations.text: The galaxy, the planets, adventures in abandoned factories, traveling through the starry night sky–all these things that cannot be seen in real life appear in front of me so easily.
Yes, yes, yes, my heart starts beating.

annotations.comment: I still love this very descriptive narrative intro! Nice job :)
annotations.context_left: smell of rain flowing in the air. The moon is already out, and the bright moonlight

annotations.comment: Nice job with verb tense, too :)
annotations.context_left: parking lot, is gone. I take a deep breath, walking past the flowers and grass. A smile

paragraphs.body: To live in games means to Escape the grame. Everyone starts the same, but all live in a frame. I have no eyes for the surroundings but my ear inside my body. "Stop playing video games" is a saying I heard often growing up. Ever since I got addicted to them, this quote never goes away. For me, video games are interesting and help me relax. But my mom is a typical Chinese parent who hates their kids being on their phones and believes that video games are the reason for everything bad. She restricted my screen time because she thought it was affecting my grades even though I got all A's in my classes. She also thought video games were the main reason why I had such bad eyes; however, I believe that video games only have a little effect on my eyes, and the main reason for my eyes being this bad was because of studying. Especially when I was a kid, I read books when the lights were off. But she blamed everything on my phone and always said to me, “I wish I never brought you a phone.” More importantly, she always watched me when I was playing, which made me very uncomfortable. She also kept yelling at me for being on my phone longer than six hours each week and kept asking me how long I had been playing. As a result, I started my first “phone war” with her, and this was the first time I lied–about video games.

annotations.text: being this bad was because of studying.

paragraphs.body: Time is passing away, And time needed to wait. I saw her crying in her bedroom, saying how my phone turned me from a good daughter into a rebellious kid. I knew I was making her sad when I didn’t want to, but I needed to stand up for myself. Everyone around me can be on their phone as long as they want to be, but why can’t I? As an always obedient kid, I told myself I had to be “mean” once, or else I knew I would never be able to have a phone again. And yet, the war was on, and I refused to stop playing games. She literally removed my door from its hinges and installed a camera in my room to prevent me from playing games on my phone. Her action enraged me. I started playing around with my computer and learning some coding skills so I could play games secretly while leaving no history behind. I started changing the app logos and names while using different methods to hide the games into different files and tables. I acted like I didn’t know anything about computers in front of her, trying to give her the illusion that I’m a computer idiot, so she wouldn’t check my computer so closely. Additionally, I discovered ways to unlock screen time and delete game time showing on my iPhone. I also irritated her by saying she couldn’t stop me from playing as long as I wanted. I could see her frustration because she tried to catch evidence of me playing games, but she couldn’t. She asked me how I did all these because she knew I was playing, but I refused to tell her. Things got worse when she confiscated my phone and expected me to get all A’s while I couldn’t use games to relax, which led to the idea of me leaving home.

annotations.comment: I'm interested in the rhythm this poetic refrain creates. It's such a cool intuitive move for the essay :)
The concrete and specific details in this story about the phone war are awesome :)
annotations.context_right: learning some coding skills so I could play games secretly while leaving no history

paragraphs.body: Worlds can be created, And break from the gate. My friends in China had the same problems as I did, but they were worse. This past summer I went to China for vacation. I met my friends and hung out for a bit. Shockingly, I noticed that their parents put them into a training class, in which they do nothing but eat, study, and sleep every single day during summer break. No rest, but continuous learning and doing problems in books.

annotations.comment: More great concrete and specific details on this page! Well done. I'm also interested in the way your essay shifts big ideas (from your own life to the lives of students in China) so fluidly and cohesively. Nice.
annotations.context_left: continuous learning and doing problems in books.

paragraphs.body: One particular friend’s whole face had countless red zits and rough and bumpy skin like a mutated monster. He told me he gave up his life outside of the classroom, studying endlessly, and finally, he got into one of the top ten colleges in China. I knew there was a lot of stress according to social media but didn’t know it was that crazy.

Meanwhile, I had another friend who was not able to hang out with us when our families had a gathering. For instance, during her mother's birthday, her mother asked her to pick between going to the party or studying for her test. And she chose to study because she knew if she didn’t, she would get a bad grade. In addition, she complained to me about how her mother locked her phone so that she couldn’t even chat with her friends for more than half an hour a day.

Many parents in China did not care about their children’s happiness; in fact, all they cared about was that their kids suceeded. Most Chinese parents were incredibly strict with their kids because they wanted their kids to be the top ones in their class. They beat their kids when they didn’t get an A. They dehumanized their kids for being so stupid and having bad school performance. Meanwhile, the school also gave them a lot of stress because everyone there knew the grades were everything for their life. If they didn’t get a good grade on the Gaokao (college entry exam), their lives were ruined. No college wanted them, and they had nowhere to go, and nowhere to stay. Their family and home were not a haven for them anymore, because they knew they would be facing yelling and disappointment. Suicide and depression were not a rare thing to see anymore. As time passed, the high expectations of their parents and fierce competition with their peers created an atmosphere that blocked many potential world changers into an endless abyss. They went straight to the window and jumped right out without hesitating. Maybe these kids just wanted to punish their parents by committing suicided. They imagined seeing the regret on their parents’ faces. What if they had just taught their kid the right way to control their phones instead of being so thorny?

annotations.text: They went straight to the window and jumped right out without hesitating. Maybe these kids just wanted to punish their parents by committing suicided.

annotations.comment: I think this paragraph should be in present tense throughout :)

paragraphs.body: All kids back in China wanted was a break from reality, and my friends and I here in the United States want the same thing. But do video games really hurt our lives? For me, no. The games led me to have an interest in computer science. It gave me happiness when I was not able to go out (such as COVID quarantine). From video games, I also learned new languages and became more and more creative as I saw different designs and fashion other people created. Games can be real too. They give you emotion and teach you lessons about the world without hurting you physically.

annotations.comment: I'd also keep this paragraph in present tense.
annotations.context_left: games, I also learned new languages and became more and more creative as I saw

paragraphs.body: “Beep beep” a very small noise catches my ear. I know what is coming. I quickly stand up and look out the window. They are back. I have to be quick. I grab my homework from my school bag and spread it all over the desk. I close up the tabs on my laptop and open Google Classroom. I open my door briskly and sit back down and pretend I’m a good kid who is just studying. Once again, I’ve tricked my mom by pretending I am doing my homework, pretending that I am her “nerd” daughter who loves school. But I don’t like high school and can never go back to that girl who is more excited about the outside world than a single laptop. But this is me.

annotations.text: “Beep beep” a very small noise catches my ear. I know what is coming. I quickly stand up and look out the window. They are back. I have to be quick. I grab

Your intro and conclusion are awesome and the way you structure the essay is intriguing and effective. And your voice is coming through with so much confidence! Well done! Thanks for sharing it. :)
